audit_status.review_note: Reviewed for Mamre visitation, hospitality language, Sarah's laughter, wonderful/impossible question, justice/righteousness, outcry of Sodom, and Abraham's intercession. Final source-critical review may refine angelic/divine identification issues.

verses.restored: The Eternal appeared to him by the oaks of Mamre, while he was sitting at the entrance of the tent in the heat of the day.

familiar.text: And the LORD appeared unto him in the plains of Mamre...

notes.title: Appeared by the oaks
notes.body: The chapter begins with a divine appearance, then immediately describes three men. The narrative blends divine and messenger presence.

verses.restored: He lifted his eyes and saw, and look, three men were standing near him. When he saw them, he ran from the entrance of the tent to meet them and bowed himself to the earth.

familiar.text: And he lift up his eyes and looked, and, lo, three men stood by him...

verses.restored: He said, My lord, if now I have found favor in your eyes, please do not pass by your servant.

familiar.text: And said, My Lord, if now I have found favour in thy sight, pass not away...

notes.title: My lord
notes.body: Adonai here may be a respectful address to the lead visitor or divine address depending on interpretation.

verses.restored: Please let a little water be brought, and wash your feet, and rest yourselves under the tree.

familiar.text: Let a little water, I pray you, be fetched, and wash your feet, and rest yourselves under the tree:

verses.restored: I will bring a morsel of bread, and strengthen your hearts; afterward you may pass on, since you have passed by your servant. They said, Do as you have spoken.

familiar.text: And I will fetch a morsel of bread, and comfort ye your hearts...

notes.title: Strengthen your hearts
notes.body: The idiom means refresh or sustain. Hospitality is bodily care.

verses.restored: Abraham hurried into the tent to Sarah and said, Quickly, three measures of fine flour; knead it and make cakes.

familiar.text: And Abraham hastened into the tent unto Sarah, and said, Make ready quickly three measures of fine meal...

verses.restored: Abraham ran to the herd, took a tender and good calf, and gave it to the young man, who hurried to prepare it.

familiar.text: And Abraham ran unto the herd, and fetcht a calf tender and good...

verses.restored: He took curds, milk, and the calf he had prepared, and set it before them. He stood by them under the tree while they ate.

familiar.text: And he took butter, and milk, and the calf which he had dressed...

verses.restored: They said to him, Where is Sarah your woman? He said, Look, in the tent.

familiar.text: And they said unto him, Where is Sarah thy wife? And he said, Behold, in the tent.

notes.title: Sarah named
notes.body: The visitors know Sarah’s covenant name, centering her in the promise scene.

verses.restored: He said, I will surely return to you at the time of life, and look, Sarah your woman shall have a son. Sarah was listening at the entrance of the tent behind him.

familiar.text: And he said, I will certainly return unto thee according to the time of life...

notes.title: Time of life
notes.body: The phrase likely means at the season/time when life comes, often understood as this time next year.

verses.restored: Now Abraham and Sarah were old, advanced in days; the way of women had ceased for Sarah.

familiar.text: Now Abraham and Sarah were old and well stricken in age; and it ceased to be with Sarah after the manner of women.

notes.title: Way of women
notes.body: A discreet idiom for menstrual/reproductive capacity. The promise is placed against biological impossibility.

verses.restored: Sarah laughed within herself, saying, After I am worn out, shall I have pleasure, and my lord is old?

familiar.text: Therefore Sarah laughed within herself, saying, After I am waxed old shall I have pleasure...

notes.title: Pleasure
notes.body: Ednah can mean pleasure, delight, or renewed vitality. Sarah’s laughter is embodied and honest.

verses.restored: The Eternal said to Abraham, Why did Sarah laugh, saying, Shall I truly bear, when I am old?

familiar.text: And the LORD said unto Abraham, Wherefore did Sarah laugh...

verses.restored: Is anything too wonderful for the Eternal? At the appointed time I will return to you, at the time of life, and Sarah shall have a son.

familiar.text: Is any thing too hard for the LORD? At the time appointed I will return...

notes.title: Too wonderful
notes.body: Pala can mean too difficult, extraordinary, or wonderful. Too wonderful preserves the wonder of impossibility.

verses.restored: Sarah denied it, saying, I did not laugh, for she was afraid. He said, No, but you did laugh.

familiar.text: Then Sarah denied, saying, I laughed not; for she was afraid. And he said, Nay; but thou didst laugh.

notes.title: Fear and laughter
notes.body: Sarah’s denial is rooted in fear. The visitor names the truth without withdrawing the promise.

verses.restored: The men rose from there and looked down toward Sodom, and Abraham went with them to send them on their way.

familiar.text: And the men rose up from thence, and looked toward Sodom...

verses.restored: The Eternal said, Shall I hide from Abraham what I am about to do,

familiar.text: And the LORD said, Shall I hide from Abraham that thing which I do;

notes.title: Divine disclosure
notes.body: Abraham is drawn into divine deliberation because of his covenant vocation.

verses.restored: since Abraham shall surely become a great and mighty nation, and all the nations of the earth shall be blessed in him?

familiar.text: Seeing that Abraham shall surely become a great and mighty nation...

verses.restored: For I have known him, so that he may command his children and his household after him to keep the way of the Eternal, to do righteousness and justice, so that the Eternal may bring upon Abraham what he has spoken concerning him.

familiar.text: For I know him, that he will command his children and his household after him...

notes.title: Righteousness and justice
notes.body: Tsedaqah and mishpat define Abraham’s vocation: not abstract privilege, but a household formed in justice.

verses.restored: The Eternal said, The outcry of Sodom and Gomorrah is great, and their offense is very heavy.

familiar.text: And the LORD said, Because the cry of Sodom and Gomorrah is great, and because their sin is very grievous;

notes.title: Outcry
notes.body: Tsa'aqah is the cry of victims or distress. Sodom’s offense is framed in relation to outcry.

verses.restored: I will go down now and see whether they have done altogether according to the outcry that has come to me; and if not, I will know.

familiar.text: I will go down now, and see whether they have done altogether according to the cry of it...

notes.title: Go down and see
notes.body: As at Babel, divine descent investigates human action. The language portrays judgment as inquiry, not impulsive reaction.

verses.restored: The men turned from there and went toward Sodom, but Abraham still stood before the Eternal.

familiar.text: And the men turned their faces from thence, and went toward Sodom: but Abraham stood yet before the LORD.

notes.title: Stood before the Eternal
notes.body: Abraham remains in intercession posture while the messengers move toward Sodom.

verses.restored: Abraham drew near and said, Will you really sweep away the righteous with the harmful?

familiar.text: And Abraham drew near, and said, Wilt thou also destroy the righteous with the wicked?

notes.title: Righteous with harmful
notes.body: Tsaddiq and rasha/ra-related moral contrast drives Abraham’s intercession: indiscriminate destruction would violate justice.

verses.restored: Perhaps there are fifty righteous within the city. Will you really sweep away and not spare the place for the sake of the fifty righteous who are within it?

familiar.text: Peradventure there be fifty righteous within the city: wilt thou also destroy and not spare...

verses.restored: Far be it from you to do such a thing, to put the righteous to death with the harmful, so that the righteous would be like the harmful. Far be it from you. Shall the Judge of all the earth not do justice?

familiar.text: That be far from thee to do after this manner... Shall not the Judge of all the earth do right?

notes.title: Judge of all the earth
notes.body: Abraham appeals to the Divine’s own justice. This is one of Genesis’s strongest intercessory justice arguments.

verses.restored: The Eternal said, If I find in Sodom fifty righteous within the city, then I will spare the whole place for their sake.

familiar.text: And the LORD said, If I find in Sodom fifty righteous within the city...

verses.restored: Abraham answered and said, Look, I have begun to speak to my Lord, though I am dust and ashes.

familiar.text: And Abraham answered and said, Behold now, I have taken upon me to speak unto the Lord...

notes.title: Dust and ashes
notes.body: Abraham approaches boldly yet humbly, naming human smallness.

verses.restored: Perhaps the fifty righteous lack five. Will you ruin the whole city for five? He said, I will not ruin it if I find forty-five there.

familiar.text: Peradventure there shall lack five of the fifty righteous...

verses.restored: He spoke to him yet again and said, Perhaps forty will be found there. He said, I will not do it for the sake of the forty.

familiar.text: And he spake unto him yet again, and said, Peradventure there shall be forty found there...

verses.restored: He said, Please let my Lord not burn with anger, and I will speak. Perhaps thirty will be found there. He said, I will not do it if I find thirty there.

familiar.text: And he said unto him, Oh let not the Lord be angry, and I will speak...

verses.restored: He said, Look, I have begun to speak to my Lord. Perhaps twenty will be found there. He said, I will not ruin it for the sake of the twenty.

familiar.text: And he said, Behold now, I have taken upon me to speak unto the Lord...

verses.restored: He said, Please let my Lord not burn with anger, and I will speak only this once. Perhaps ten will be found there. He said, I will not ruin it for the sake of the ten.

familiar.text: And he said, Oh let not the Lord be angry, and I will speak yet but this once...

notes.title: For the sake of ten
notes.body: The intercession reaches ten righteous. The logic is that a righteous remnant can preserve a whole place.

verses.restored: The Eternal went away when he had finished speaking to Abraham, and Abraham returned to his place.

familiar.text: And the LORD went his way, as soon as he had left communing with Abraham...
